questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 3-week history of limping and intermittent right hip pain. He is afebrile and has no history of trauma. On examination, there is limited abduction and internal rotation of the right hip. Radiograph shows flattening and increased density of the right femoral head. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Classic age (4–8 years), chronic limp, no fever, and radiographic evidence of femoral head flattening and sclerosis are characteristic of Perthes disease. Transient synovitis is acute and self-limiting with normal radiographs.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with bilateral hip and knee pain of 2 months' duration, associated with morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes. She is otherwise well and afebrile. Radiographs show bilateral femoral head flattening and fragmentation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bilateral Perthes occurs in 10–20% of cases. The radiographic appearance of femoral head necrosis with flattening and fragmentation is typical. JIA usually shows systemic features or other joint involvement, and SUFE presents with epiphyseal displacement, not collapse.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy complains of persistent right knee pain for 4 weeks. He has no knee swelling or warmth, and knee examination is normal. However, hip examination reveals limited internal rotation and abduction. He walks with a slight limp. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Knee pain is a classic referred symptom from hip pathology. Normal knee exam plus hip findings (limited internal rotation) in a 5-year-old make Perthes the most likely. Osgood-Schlatter typically causes tibial tuberosity tenderness.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with a limp for 8 weeks, initially thought to be transient synovitis. He has no fever. Radiographs of the hip are normal. An MRI shows reduced perfusion of the femoral head epiphysis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In early Perthes, radiographs may be normal. MRI demonstrating avascular necrosis (reduced perfusion) is diagnostic. Transient synovitis would not show perfusion defects and typically resolves within weeks. Septic arthritis presents acutely with fever.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old girl presents with a 3-month history of left groin pain and limp. She is otherwise healthy and not overweight. Examination shows limited abduction and internal rotation. Radiograph reveals femoral head collapse and sclerosis, with a crescent sign. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Avascular necrosis secondary to steroid use
questions.explanation: Although Perthes is less common over age 10, the radiographic features of femoral head collapse and crescent sign (subchondral fracture) are characteristic. SUFE shows epiphyseal displacement, not collapse. No history of steroid use or systemic illness makes AVN due to other causes less likely.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with a limp and right hip pain for 2 weeks. There is no fever. On examination, hip range of motion is limited, especially abduction and internal rotation. What is the single most appropriate initial investigation?
questions.explanation: X-ray is the first-line investigation for suspected Perthes disease, showing changes such as increased density, fragmentation, or collapse. Ultrasound may show effusion but is not diagnostic; MRI is reserved for equivocal cases.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl has a 1-month history of mild left hip pain and a limp. Initial X-ray of the hip is reported as normal. What is the best next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: MRI is sensitive for early Perthes disease, detecting avascular necrosis before X-ray changes appear. Repeat X-ray would delay diagnosis; ultrasound is non-diagnostic.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with acute onset of severe left hip pain and inability to weight bear. X-ray shows a crescent-shaped subchondral lucency in the femoral head. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The crescent sign (subchondral fracture) is a classic early radiographic sign of Perthes disease, representing a pathologic fracture through necrotic bone.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with bilateral hip pain and limping. X-rays show flattening and fragmentation of both femoral heads. Which condition is most likely?
questions.explanation: Bilateral symmetric involvement of the femoral heads is atypical for Perthes (usually unilateral) and suggests a skeletal dysplasia such as multiple epiphyseal dysplasia. Sickle cell can cause bilateral AVN but typically shows different X-ray features.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old child has suspected early Perthes disease on clinical grounds. An MRI is performed. Which MRI finding is most specific for confirming the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The subchondral fracture line (crescent sign) on MRI represents a pathologic fracture through necrotic bone and is highly specific for Perthes disease. Bone marrow oedema is non-specific.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 2-week history of right hip pain and a limp. Examination reveals mild restriction of internal rotation. Radiographs show early Perthes disease (Waldenström stage I) with no collapse. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Conservative management with activity modification and analgesia
questions.explanation: In early Perthes disease, especially in younger children (<6 years), the mainstay is conservative management to maintain range of motion and containment. Surgery is reserved for older children or those with poor prognostic factors.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl with known Perthes disease in the fragmentation stage has good containment of the femoral head on radiographs and mild symptoms. What is the most appropriate definitive therapy?
questions.explanation: In the fragmentation stage with good containment, an abduction brace helps maintain containment during healing. Surgery is considered if containment is lost or the child is older.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old boy with Perthes disease presents with severe hip pain and inability to weight bear. Radiographs show a 'head at risk' (Gage sign, lateral calcification). What is the most appropriate first-line surgical treatment?
questions.explanation: In older children with poor prognostic features, proximal femoral varus osteotomy improves containment by rotating the femoral head into the acetabulum, reducing the risk of deformity.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old with Perthes disease develops progressive hip stiffness and pain. MRI reveals hinge abduction due to femoral head deformity. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Valgus extension osteotomy of the proximal femur
questions.explanation: Hinge abduction occurs when the deformed femoral head impinges in abduction. A valgus extension osteotomy moves the deformed segment out of the weight-bearing area, relieving pain and improving range of motion.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old with healed Perthes disease (Catterall group II, spherical femoral head, good range of motion) is asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate long-term management?
options: Total hip arthroplasty at skeletal maturity
questions.explanation: For Perthes disease with good healing and no symptoms, no further intervention is needed. The risk of early osteoarthritis is low in Catterall group II, so routine follow-up is unnecessary.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 2-week history of right hip pain and limp. He is afebrile. Examination reveals limited internal rotation of the right hip. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Blood tests including ESR and CRP
questions.explanation: X-ray is the first-line investigation for suspected Perthes disease, as it can show early changes such as increased joint space or subchondral fracture. Ultrasound may show effusion but is not diagnostic. Blood tests are for infection, but fever is absent. Referral follows imaging. Rest and analgesia alone are insufficient for diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl with newly diagnosed Perthes disease (lateral pillar A, no collapse) has mild pain and a limp. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Urgent orthopaedic referral for varus osteotomy
Reassure and discharge with follow-up in 6 months
Schedule an MRI scan to assess containment
questions.explanation: MRI is recommended by NICE to assess femoral head containment and guide management in active Perthes disease. Surgery is not urgent in mild disease. Physiotherapy is conservative but MRI helps decide if containment needed. Bed rest is overly restrictive. Discharge without staging is inappropriate.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy with known Perthes disease (stage II) presents with acute worsening of hip pain and inability to weight-bear after a minor fall. He is afebrile. X-ray shows femoral head collapse with lateral extrusion. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform hip aspiration to rule out septic arthritis
Prescribe strong analgesia and arrange physiotherapy
Apply traction and keep nil by mouth
Urgent referral to paediatric orthopaedics for possible containment surgery
questions.explanation: Acute collapse with extrusion is a complication requiring urgent surgical containment (e.g., varus osteotomy or shelf acetabuloplasty) to preserve hip congruity. Traction is not standard. Analgesia and physiotherapy are insufficient. No signs of infection; aspiration not indicated. Non-weight-bearing alone is inadequate for this severity.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old child with a history of Perthes disease is now asymptomatic for 2 years. Follow-up X-ray shows a healed femoral head with mild flattening but good containment. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Prescribe calcium and vitamin D supplements
questions.explanation: Once Perthes disease is healed and the child is asymptomatic, no further follow-up is required. NICE advises discharge. Annual MRI and arthroscopy are not indicated. Activity restriction is unnecessary. Calcium and vitamin D are not specific for Perthes.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl presents with acute left hip pain and fever (38.5°C). She refuses to weight-bear. Blood tests show raised white cell count and CRP. X-ray of both hips is normal. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Obtain an MRI scan of the hips
questions.explanation: In a febrile child with raised inflammatory markers and hip pain, septic arthritis must be excluded urgently. Hip ultrasound with aspiration is the next step to obtain synovial fluid for culture. Antibiotics should be given after cultures are taken. Orthopaedic referral follows aspiration. NSAIDs may mask infection. MRI is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with a limp and right hip pain for 1 day. He is afebrile and playful. Hip examination shows minimal restriction of internal rotation. Which single finding would most strongly suggest septic arthritis rather than Perthes disease?
questions.explanation: Fever >38.5°C is a red flag for septic arthritis, which must be excluded urgently with aspiration. Perthes disease typically presents without fever.

questions.stem: An 11-year-old obese boy presents with a limp and right knee pain. On hip examination, when the hip is flexed, it externally rotates and internal rotation is limited. Which diagnosis must be excluded?
questions.explanation: The classic finding of external rotation on flexion and limited internal rotation is pathognomonic for slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE). SCFE is a surgical emergency to prevent avascular necrosis.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl presents with bilateral hip pain and stiffness. On examination, both hips have reduced internal rotation and abduction. Which additional finding would prompt investigation for an underlying systemic condition?
questions.explanation: Bilateral Perthes disease can be associated with endocrinopathies such as hypothyroidism or skeletal dysplasias. Short stature (height <3rd centile) is a key alert to investigate further.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy with known Perthes disease in the left hip (fragmentation stage) is in a brace. He reports increasing pain and difficulty with sports. On examination, the left hip has a block to abduction when internally rotated. This finding signifies which complication?
questions.explanation: A block to abduction with internal rotation indicates hinge abduction, where the deformed femoral head catches on the acetabular rim. This requires orthopaedic assessment for possible surgical intervention.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl presents with a limp and hip pain for 2 weeks. X-ray of both hips is normal. On examination, which finding would most strongly suggest early Perthes disease and warrant MRI?
questions.explanation: In early Perthes, the earliest clinical sign is painful limitation of internal rotation. An MRI can detect avascular necrosis before X-ray changes appear, enabling early treatment.
